event: Neolithic farming communities at Mehrgarh begin cultivating crops and domesticating animals, marking the earliest settled life in the subcontinent.
The Indus Valley (Harappan) Civilization emerges, developing planned cities, drainage systems, and standardized weights across the northwest.
Major Harappan urban centers such as Mohenjo-daro and Harappa flourish as one of the world's earliest great urban civilizations.
The Indus Valley Civilization declines as major cities are gradually abandoned amid climate change and shifting river patterns.
Indo-Aryan peoples and Vedic culture spread across northern India, beginning composition of the Rigveda and the Vedic tradition.
The later Vedic period formalizes social hierarchies, ritual practice, and the early caste system across the Gangetic plain.
The Mahajanapadas, sixteen powerful kingdoms and republics, arise across northern India during the second urbanization.
Siddhartha Gautama, the future Buddha, is born, founding a tradition that would become Buddhism.
Mahavira, the 24th Tirthankara, revitalizes Jainism, shaping a major Indian religious and philosophical tradition.
Alexander the Great invades the Punjab and defeats King Porus at the Battle of the Hydaspes before withdrawing from India.
Chandragupta Maurya founds the Maurya Empire, the first to unify most of the Indian subcontinent under a single ruler.
Ashoka the Great ascends the Mauryan throne and, after the bloody Kalinga War, embraces Buddhism and spreads it across Asia.
The Maurya Empire collapses and is succeeded by the Shunga dynasty in northern India.
The Saka era calendar begins under Kushan ruler Kanishka, who presides over a flourishing of Buddhism and Silk Road commerce.
Chandragupta I founds the Gupta Empire, inaugurating a classical golden age of Indian science, mathematics, and art.
Mathematician Aryabhata composes the Aryabhatiya, advancing astronomy, trigonometry, and the concept of zero and place value.
The Gupta Empire disintegrates under pressure from the Huna invasions, fragmenting northern India into regional kingdoms.
Harsha establishes a short-lived empire over much of northern India, becoming a noted patron of learning and Buddhism.
Umayyad general Muhammad bin Qasim conquers Sindh, marking the first significant Muslim foothold in the subcontinent.
The Chola dynasty rises to prominence in southern India, later building a powerful maritime empire across the Indian Ocean.
Mahmud of Ghazni begins a series of raids into northern India, plundering wealthy temple cities.
Muhammad of Ghor defeats Prithviraj Chauhan at the Second Battle of Tarain, opening northern India to Muslim rule.
Qutb al-Din Aibak founds the Delhi Sultanate, beginning over three centuries of Turkic and Afghan rule in northern India.
The Vijayanagara Empire is founded in southern India, becoming a major Hindu power and center of culture.
Timur sacks Delhi, devastating the city and hastening the decline of the Delhi Sultanate.
Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama reaches Calicut, opening a direct sea route between Europe and India.
Babur defeats Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat and founds the Mughal Empire.
Akbar the Great ascends the throne and expands the Mughal Empire while promoting religious tolerance and administrative reform.
The English East India Company is chartered, beginning English commercial penetration of India.
Emperor Shah Jahan begins construction of the Taj Mahal at Agra in memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal.
Shivaji founds the Maratha Empire, which would rise to challenge Mughal dominance across much of India.
The death of Emperor Aurangzeb triggers the gradual decline and fragmentation of the Mughal Empire.
Robert Clive's victory at the Battle of Plassey establishes British East India Company dominance over Bengal.
The Indian Rebellion (Sepoy Mutiny) erupts against East India Company rule, the first major uprising for Indian independence.
The British Crown assumes direct rule of India, ending Company governance and establishing the British Raj.
The Indian National Congress is founded, becoming the leading vehicle of the independence movement.
British troops massacre hundreds of unarmed civilians at Jallianwala Bagh in Amritsar, galvanizing nationalist sentiment.
Mahatma Gandhi leads the Salt March, a landmark act of nonviolent civil disobedience against British rule.
Gandhi launches the Quit India Movement, demanding an immediate end to British rule during World War II.
India gains independence on 15 August and is partitioned into India and Pakistan, accompanied by mass migration and violence.
Mahatma Gandhi is assassinated by a Hindu nationalist in New Delhi.
India adopts its Constitution and becomes a sovereign democratic republic on 26 January.
India suffers a defeat in a brief border war with China in the Himalayas.
India and Pakistan fight a major war over the disputed region of Kashmir.
India intervenes in the Bangladesh Liberation War, defeating Pakistan and aiding the creation of Bangladesh.
India conducts its first nuclear test, code-named Smiling Buddha, at Pokhran.
Prime Minister Indira Gandhi declares a national State of Emergency, suspending civil liberties for nearly two years.
Indira Gandhi is assassinated by her bodyguards following Operation Blue Star, and the Bhopal gas disaster kills thousands.
India launches sweeping economic liberalization reforms, opening its economy and accelerating growth.
India conducts the Pokhran-II nuclear tests, openly declaring itself a nuclear weapons state.
Coordinated terrorist attacks in Mumbai kill over 160 people, straining India-Pakistan relations.
Narendra Modi leads the BJP to a decisive election victory, becoming prime minister.
India's Chandrayaan-3 mission lands near the lunar south pole, and the country becomes the world's most populous nation.
